g.data-narration: Welcome to Multithreaded Programming! This topic covers threads, their benefits, and how parallel execution works.
A thread is the basic unit of CPU utilization. Each thread has its own PC, registers, and stack, while sharing code, data, and files with other threads.
Compare single-threaded (one execution path) vs multi-threaded (multiple tasks simultaneously) processes. Notice how threads share resources.
Web servers use multithreading to handle multiple client requests efficiently. Creating a thread is much faster than creating a new process.
Multithreading offers four key benefits: Responsiveness, Resource Sharing, Economy, and Scalability. Remember RRES!
Thread creation is 30x faster than process creation in Solaris. Context switching between threads is also 5x faster.
Concurrency means tasks make progress (may interleave). Parallelism means simultaneous execution on multiple cores. Both are different!
g.text: Interleaved on single core - tasks make progress
Simultaneous on multiple cores - true parallel

g.data-narration: Five multicore challenges: Dividing activities, Balance, Data splitting, Data dependency, and Testing/debugging.
Data parallelism: Same operation on distributed data subsets. Example: summing array parts on different cores simultaneously.
g.text: Same operation on distributed data subsets

g.data-narration: Task parallelism: Different operations on separate cores. Each thread performs a unique task, like sorting vs computing statistics.
Data parallelism: Same operation, distributed data. Task parallelism: Different operations on separate cores. Key distinction for exams!
Amdahls Law shows maximum speedup limited by serial portion. Formula: Speedup = 1 / (S + (1-S)/N). Even infinite cores cant exceed 1/S!
g.text: Maximum speedup limited by serial portion

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Threads share resources but have own execution context. Four benefits RRES. Concurrency vs parallelism. Amdahls Law limits speedup.
g.text: Thread = Basic CPU unit (PC, Reg, Stack); shares code/data/files
Benefits: Responsiveness, Resource Sharing, Economy, Scalability (RRES)
Data Parallelism: same op on split data | Task Parallelism: different ops
Amdahls Law: Speedup limited by serial portion - 1/(S + (1-S)/N)
